warnings.message: Fluoroquinolones may cause severe blood sugar fluctuations (hypo/hyperglycemia) in diabetic patients. Monitor glucose closely.
Increased risk of tendon rupture and peripheral neuropathy in elderly patients. Consider alternatives if available.
Fluoroquinolones are generally contraindicated in children — risk of cartilage and joint damage during growth.
warnings.source: WHO Essential Medicines List for Children

warnings.message: Fluoroquinolones may cause dysglycemia in diabetic patients. Monitor blood sugar carefully during therapy.
Elderly patients face elevated risk of tendon rupture with fluoroquinolone use. Prefer safer alternatives when possible.
Fluoroquinolones are generally contraindicated in pediatric patients due to risk of musculoskeletal toxicity.
warnings.source: WHO Essential Medicines List for Children

warnings.message: Nalidixic acid is considered obsolete for empiric therapy in most current guidelines. Newer quinolones are preferred.
Nalidixic acid should be avoided in young children — risk of increased intracranial pressure and cartilage toxicity.
Colistin is nephrotoxic. Renal function (creatinine, GFR) must be assessed before and during therapy. Hydrate aggressively.
Diabetic patients have elevated baseline renal risk. Colistin-induced nephrotoxicity may be more severe. Monitor renal function closely.
Elderly patients have reduced renal clearance. Colistin dose adjustment and close monitoring of renal function is essential.
Aminoglycosides carry risk of nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity. Monitor renal function and drug levels during therapy.
Elderly patients require dose adjustment for Gentamicin based on creatinine clearance to avoid nephro/ototoxicity.
Amikacin (aminoglycoside) carries risk of nephrotoxicity and irreversible ototoxicity. Therapeutic drug monitoring is recommended.
Renal dose adjustment required in elderly patients. Monitor serum creatinine and amikacin trough levels.
Imipenem may lower the seizure threshold. Use with caution in patients with CNS disorders or renal impairment.
Dose reduction required in elderly/renal-impaired patients to reduce CNS toxicity (seizures).
Penicillin allergy is common (8-10% of patients). Always confirm absence of penicillin/beta-lactam allergy before prescribing.
Contains penicillin (amoxicillin). Confirm absence of penicillin allergy. Risk of hepatotoxicity with prolonged use.
Elderly patients are at higher risk of cholestatic jaundice with amoxicillin-clavulanate. Prefer shorter courses.
Cross-reactivity between penicillin and 1st-gen cephalosporins is ~1-2%. Confirm absence of severe penicillin allergy.
Cephamycins have low cross-reactivity with penicillin, but caution is advised in patients with severe beta-lactam allergy.
Ceftriaxone is contraindicated in neonates receiving IV calcium — risk of fatal cardiopulmonary precipitation.
Ceftriaxone may cause biliary sludging/pseudolithiasis, particularly with prolonged use. Monitor clinically.
Chloramphenicol carries risk of dose-independent aplastic anemia (rare but fatal). Reserve for serious infections when no alternatives exist.
Contraindicated in neonates — risk of Gray Baby Syndrome (cardiovascular collapse from immature hepatic metabolism).
Co-trimoxazole can cause hyperkalemia and renal toxicity in elderly patients. Check potassium and renal function before prescribing.
Inhibits folate metabolism. Use with caution in patients with pre-existing folate deficiency or on antifolate drugs (e.g., methotrexate).
Nitrofurantoin is ineffective if creatinine clearance is below 30 mL/min (common in elderly). Check GFR before prescribing.
Prolonged use may cause pulmonary fibrosis. Not recommended for long-term prophylaxis in patients with lung disease.
